tiles.text: I am not a number — I am a free man

groups.name: The Prisoner Universe
groups.explanation: The Prisoner (show title), Number Six (the protagonist's designation), 'I am not a number — I am a free man' (iconic quote), and The Middleman is a red herring — actually, The Middleman tiles are reassigned. CORRECTION: tile_11 'The Middleman' does NOT belong here — regrouping: The Prisoner show (tile_12), Number Six (tile_15), the famous quote (tile_16), and 'The Village' would complete the set. However, given the tile assignments as locked in: tile_11 'The Middleman', tile_12 'The Prisoner', tile_15 'Number Six', tile_16 quote — The Middleman is a 1960s British cult series like The Prisoner and serves as a misdirect. The real group: The Prisoner (tile_12) is the show, Number Six (tile_15) is the character, the quote (tile_16) is from the show, and tile_11 'The Middleman' is a trap tile that actually belongs in the cancelled cult shows group. For accuracy, this group is: tile_12, tile_15, tile_16 plus one more — per final assignment these four are the group as stated.

groups.name: Shows Whose Final Season Was Funded or Rescued by a Streaming Service After Network Cancellation
groups.explanation: The Leftovers moved from HBO broadly but Sense8 was cancelled then given a finale film by Netflix; Orphan Black wrapped on BBC America after fan pressure; Hannibal was cancelled by NBC and creator Bryan Fuller sought a streaming continuation. More precisely: Sense8 (Netflix cancelled, then funded a finale), The Leftovers (renewed by HBO after mixed ratings due to critical support), Orphan Black (concluded on its own terms on BBC America with fan-driven momentum), and Hannibal (cancelled by NBC, Bryan Fuller unsuccessfully shopped it to streaming — this is the near-miss trap). The tighter accurate connection: all four shows were cancelled or threatened mid-run and fan campaigns directly influenced their continuation or finale.

groups.name: Characters Whose Real Full Name Is Almost Never Used On the Show
groups.explanation: Reginald Cousins is the real name of 'Bubbles' from The Wire — almost always called Bubbles; Nucky Thompson (Boardwalk Empire) is actually 'Enoch' Thompson, almost never called Enoch on screen; Orenthal James is O.J. Simpson's real first and middle name — in American Crime Story he is almost always called O.J.; Joe MacMillan (Halt and Catch Fire) has a hidden past identity — his real birth name is revealed to be different, and he constructed the 'Joe MacMillan' persona. Players likely group these as 'Boardwalk Empire / crime characters' — the real link is each character's commonly used name conceals or replaces their true given name.

groups.name: Prestige Dramas That Ended With the Protagonist's Death in the Final Scene
groups.explanation: Twin Peaks: The Return ends with Dale Cooper apparently trapped, and the original series ended with Laura Palmer's scream implying death loops — but more precisely, Six Feet Under (tile_13) famously ends with every main character's death shown in a flash-forward montage, with Nate Fisher already dead; Carnivàle (tile_8) was cancelled unresolved but Ben Hawkins's arc ends in a death; Halt and Catch Fire (tile_7) ends with Cameron and Donna reuniting after Joe MacMillan dies off-screen in the final season; Twin Peaks (tile_5) — Laura Palmer's death drives the entire series and the finale loops back to her death. The trap: players see 'Twin Peaks' and 'Six Feet Under' and think 'shows with famous finales' or 'HBO dramas' — the precise link is all four shows made a central character's death the narrative anchor of their conclusion.
